thread.title: Platte Valley school resource officer becomes Kersey’s new police chief
thread.title_full: Platte Valley school resource officer becomes Kersey’s new police chief

author: Morgan McKenzie
title: Platte Valley school resource officer becomes Kersey’s new police chief
text:
A Platte Valley RE-7 school resource officer with strong ties to the community will take on a new role of leading the Kersey Police Department.
The town of Kersey swore in Jonathan Lange last week as the new chief of police following Tracy Moore’s departure.
Moore had served as chief since 2021 when the chief before him, David Gottschalk, retired. Moore will retire to spend more time with his family and grandson.
Lange’s law enforcement career started in 2007 at the Evans Police Department. After seven years, he accepted a role at the Weld County Sheriff’s Office from 2016-22. He then worked for Central Square, a public safety software company, for about a year and a half before he became a school resource officer (SRO) in the Platte Valley RE-7 School District.
“While he’s got a plethora of experience in his background at other agencies, he was our SRO officer, so we did want to make sure that we maintain those relationships,” Town Manager Stacy Brown said. “With the school but also the fire department. We all have to work together for emergency preparedness, emergency planning, response times and all of those things.”
Lange always wanted to pursue a higher level in law enforcement, even shadowing Moore over the summer. Through Moore’s mentorship, Lange realized his love for leading the department.
Lange has built up his foundation to step up as a leader over the past 15 years, working as a patrol officer, a field training officer, a Weld County Regional SWAT team member, a Critical Incident Response Team member and more. His time as a school resource officer has also helped him grow with the community he serves.
“That gave me the opportunity and a gateway to really grow with this community through the school district,” Lange said about his former role. “It gave me a lot of insight as to what their expectations are as a community … of the chief of police here.”
A unique leadership role Lange’s top priorities as chief include ensuring the department stays compliant with expectations from the state, focusing on building relationships with the community, ensuring he meets the community’s expectations and remaining hyper-vigilant about crime-related issues in Kersey. He also wants to focus on supporting business owners.
“We want to show these business owners and be able to market through them to other ones that want to come here, that the police department has their back,” Lange said. “We’re out and about, not just enforcing tickets or taking traffic accidents, but we’re out forming those relationships with our citizens and building those community relations.”
Lange described the Kersey police chief role as “non-traditional” because he must handle administrative needs and work 12-hour patrol shifts due to the small staff on deck. The department has four full-time police officers and four reserve part-time police officers, operating with a chief, a patrol sergeant, five patrol officers and a part-time criminal investigator, according to the website.
“It’s a very unique position,” Lange said. “That was a huge driving factor … being able to stay involved with the community, being out on the streets, patrolling, talking to people and just really getting a grasp on their needs and continuing forming those relationships.”
But Lange looks forward to “the challenge.”
Only three people applied for the job due to its “unique” responsibilities,” according to Brown. Two candidates moved forward to one-on-one meetings with the police department and a public meet-and-greet where community members could leave feedback.
Most of the community feedback supported Lange for chief. With that support as well as his skills and background, Brown felt confident in recommending Lange to the board, who unanimously supported the decision.
Brown felt he had key traits that made him the best fit, including thoughtfulness, professionalism, motivation, responsiveness and impressive communication skills. He also brings a different spin to his job as someone who worked in the private and public sectors, making him more of a “well-rounded individual,” according to Brown.
“He’s got a myriad of different experiences,” Brown said. “While none of it has been as a chief of police … that didn’t matter. Leadership is leadership. What I was looking for was somebody who had the ability, which he definitely does, to step into that role. And a huge desire to do it.”
In the new position, Lange enjoys the ability to serve as a role model and mentor to the other officers and grow relationships with the town board and manager. He believes he can help officers grow their careers as they help grow his — “the beauty of a very diverse” police department.
Brown said her decision to select Lange also depended on the department’s small staff, “a tight-knit family.” The department needs to have a healthy working culture, to have each other’s backs and to trust one another. And Lange will accomplish this, Brown said.
“He has just really been a great asset to us, even in his short one-week time,” Brown said. “I have all faith that he will continue to do that.”
